key_insight: Intrinsic ductility in metals and alloys can be predicted by an amorphization-based fracture criterion, linking electronic structure and shear effects to ductile-to-brittle behavior.
engineering_relevance: The framework enables rapid screening and design of strong, room-temperature ductile structural alloys, including multi-principal-element systems like Nb-Ta-V-Ti.
method: Replace cleavage/dislocation-based fracture assumptions with activation energy density for amorphization.
Derive analytical structure-property relationships from valence charge distribution, band filling, and shear strain effects.
Use ab initio stiffness constants, lattice parameters, and binary interaction energies to build unified predictions.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports accurate predictions of intrinsic ductility and ductile-to-brittle transition temperatures, and phase diagrams for the Nb-Ta-V-Ti system align with observed high strength and room-temperature tensile ductility.
watch_item: The approach depends on reliable ab initio inputs and tabulated material parameters, so prediction quality may degrade for poorly characterized alloys or complex microstructures.
paper_title: Intrinsic Ductility from Shear Amorphization: From Pure Metals to Multi-Principal-Element Alloys
